coredata.dc:title: Filter-assisted smartphone colorimetry / ICP-MS dual-mode biosensor of butyrylcholinesterase in clinical samples
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Qiuyue
Guo, Qi
Zhou, Jinrong
Yuan, Xin
Huang, Ke
Chen, Piaopiao
coredata.dc:description: Clinically, butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) is an indispensable biochemical marker for evaluating liver function and organophosphorus poisoning. In this work, we proposed a novel smartphone readable colorimetry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) dual-mode analytical strategy for BChE detection in clinical patients based on the phenomenon that the filter membrane can selectively separate MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and Mn2+. In the presence of BChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh), the enzymatic hydrolysate (thiocholine) can efficiently trigger the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. After selective separation of MnO2 NSs and Mn2+ via the filter, colorimetric detection was achieved with a smartphone by identifying the RGB value of the MnO2 NSs coated membrane, while the Mn2+ in the filtrate was detected by ICP-MS. Under optimal conditions, the limits of detection (LODs) were as low as 1.3 U/L and 0.001 U/L for the smartphone-based RGB analysis and ICP-MS assay, respectively. This method was applied to quantify BChE in 40 clinical whole blood samples of patients with clinical liver disease. The levels of BChE detected in clinical samples by this method were consistent with clinical Ellman’s colorimetric findings. Therefore, this method can provide more options for clinical cholinesterase detection and is expected to be developed into a kit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Butyrylcholinesterase
MnO2 nanosheets
Dual-mode
Filter membrane
Clinical samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of dual-mode assay for BChE and parathion detection
Optimization of conditions for BChE assay

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605108 ), Sichuan Science and Technology Program ( 2021YFS0232 ). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . (A) SEM of MnO2 NSs. (B) XRD of MnO2 NSs. (C) UV–vis spectra of MnO2 NSs. (D) Raman spectra of MnO2 NSs. (E and F) XPS images of MnO2 NSs before reaction. (G and H) XPS images of MnO2 NSs after the reaction.

. (A) UV–vis absorption of MnO2, MnO2 + ATCh, MnO2 + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE (75 U/L) + parathion (50 μg/mL). (B) R, G, B signal comparison. (C) G/(R + G + B) intensity of MnO2, MnO2 + ATCh, MnO2 + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE + parathion, inset is corresponding filter membrane color. (D) ICP-MS signal of MnO2, MnO2 + ATCh, MnO2 + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE, MnO2 + ATCh + BChE (1 U/L) + parathion (1 μg/mL). Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.

. (A) The color change of membrane in the range of 0–150 U/L BChE. (B) Analytical performance of smartphone detection BChE. (C) Analytical performance of ICP-MS detection BChE. (D) Specific performance analysis of BChE. Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.
(A) Comparative analysis of clinical diagnosis and the detection of real samples by this method. (B) Pictures of filter membranes for testing real samples.

. (A) The color change of membrane in the range of 0–70 μg/mL parathion. (B) Analytical performance of smartphone detection parathion. (C) Analytical performance of ICP-MS detection parathion. (D) Specific performance analysis of parathion. Error bars originated from three repeated measurements.
Schematic diagram of smartphone colorimetric / ICP-MS detection of BChE and parathion.
BChE concentration of serum samples from patients and healthy population (U/L).

head.ce:title: Filter-assisted smartphone colorimetry / ICP-MS dual-mode biosensor of butyrylcholinesterase in clinical samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clinically, butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) is an indispensable biochemical marker for evaluating liver function and organophosphorus poisoning. In this work, we proposed a novel smartphone readable colorimetry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) dual-mode analytical strategy for BChE detection in clinical patients based on the phenomenon that the filter membrane can selectively separate MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and Mn2+. In the presence of BChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh), the enzymatic hydrolysate (thiocholine) can efficiently trigger the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. After selective separation of MnO2 NSs and Mn2+ via the filter, colorimetric detection was achieved with a smartphone by identifying the RGB value of the MnO2 NSs coated membrane, while the Mn2+ in the filtrate was detected by ICP-MS. Under optimal conditions, the limits of detection (LODs) were as low as 1.3 U/L and 0.001 U/L for the smartphone-based RGB analysis and ICP-MS assay, respectively. This method was applied to quantify BChE in 40 clinical whole blood samples of patients with clinical liver disease. The levels of BChE detected in clinical samples by this method were consistent with clinical Ellman’s colorimetric findings. Therefore, this method can provide more options for clinical cholinesterase detection and is expected to be developed into a kit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE: EC 3.1.1.8) is one of the most vital cholinesterases for cholinergic neurotransmission [1]. BChE is mainly existed in blood plasma and can catalyze the hydrolysis of choline-based esters like acetylcholine in a nonspecific manner [2]. The BChE activity could show the liver status and its protein production capacity. Therefore, it was utilized as a valuable biomarker in clinical diagnoses such as chronic liver disease, liver cancer, and organophosphorus toxicity [3,4]. A diverse of diseases, including chronic liver disease, liver cancer, and organophosphorus toxicity, could be opportunely and accurately diagnosed by detecting BChE activity in blood serum [5,6]. Therefore, it is important to establish a simple, rapid, and low-cost method for BChE activity assay in clinical diagnosis. Initially, the main diagnostic test for BChE activity detection in clinical laboratories was based on Ellman’s colorimetric method, which not only was insensitive but also had significant interference from molecules in serum [7,8]. Up to now, many methods have been reported including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9], chemiluminescence [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [11], electrochemical methods [12–14], Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) [15], colorimetric [16–18], and fluorescence methods for BChE assay [19–22]. Among them, nanomaterial-based colorimetry was simple, rapid, and low-cost analytical method [23,24]. In our previous work, a colorimetric method was realized for cholinesterase activity and inhibitors assay by enzymatic reaction modulated CdTe quantum dots synthesis [25]. However, only semi-quantitatively evaluation can be established by a visual readout of the originated color in comparison to a reference in colorimetric analysis. For quantitates analysis, a fluorescence spectrophotometer is still required [26–28].

The smartphone may be an ideal tool to provide simple and fast detectable readouts due to it is easy to operate in miniaturization and low-cost. Recently, several biosensing methods were reported based on the smartphone because they can transform the colors to RGB (red / green / blue) or CIE values [29–31]. For example, Shi et al. developed a new sample output platform combining ratiometric fluorimetry with a 3D origami microfluidic paper-based device for the detection of BChE by linking smartphones to fluorimetry [32]. Although smartphone-based colorimetry is convenient, the sensitivity remains to be improved [33,34]. And the BChE activity detected in real patients is often from hundreds to tens of thousands, which often exceeds the linear range of the colorimetric method. Atomic spectroscopy (AS) has a wide linear range, high sensitivity, and strong anti-interference ability for element assay [35–37]. Recently, researchers explored various bioanalysis methods of AS by monitoring the concentration of ions via the interactions of biomolecules with metal ions or nanomaterials, making AS techniques exhibit high accuracy and selectivity in the bioanalysis [38–40]. Taking into consideration both sensitivity and portability, the dual-mode analysis method was considered as an effective approach to play the strengths of both smartphone-based analysis and AS analysis. In our previous work, a series of dual-mode or multi-mode bioanalysis strategies were reported successfully.

40 cases samples of patients with liver disease and healthy volunteers were obtained from the West China Hospital of Sichuan University (approval number: 2019203). The serum of patients was diluted 100 times by phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.4), and then centrifuged for 15 min with a 100 kDa ultrafiltration tube (8000 rpm) to remove the macromolecules. The ultrafiltrates were stored at 4 °C. Finally, ATCh, MnO2 NSs, and PBS were added for the spiked-recovery experiment and detected the BChE activity in the samples.

Firstly, 500 μL various concentrations of BChE, 500 μL 5 mM of ATCh, and 500 μL 10 mM of PBS were mixed at 37 °C for 25 min. Then, 500 μL of MnO2 NSs (0.5 mg/mL) was injected and incubated for 6 min at 37 °C. Finally, the mixed solution was filtered by filter membrane, and RGB analysis was performed for the filter film color change via a smartphone. Mn2+ in filtered solution was detected by ICP-MS after diluting with 2% HNO3 solution. All measurements were made at least three times.

Briefly, various concentrations of parathion (500 μL) and BChE (500 μL) were mixed for 30 min before adding 5 mM of ATCh (500 μL) and 10 mM of PBS (500 μL) for 25 min. Then, 500 μL of MnO2 NSs (0.5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 6 min. Finally, the mixed solution was filtered, and RGB analysis was performed on the color change of the filter membrane through a smartphone. Mn2+ was detected by ICP-MS after the filtrate was diluted with 2% HNO3 solution.

MnO2 NSs were prepared using KMnO4 as the metal source and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as the reducing agent and structural inducer [41]. In order to study the morphology and characteristics of MnO2 NSs, the following characterizations were carried out. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showed that the prepared MnO2 has a uniform two-dimensional sheet structure ( Fig. 1 A). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MnO2 NSs were shown in Fig. 1B. The diffraction peaks at 12.3º, 24.8º, 36.6º, 37.4º, and 65.8º corresponded to standard pattern (PDF# 86–0666) of MnO2, indicating the prepared MnO2 NSs crystallized well [42]. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption peak around 374 nm found in the UV-Vis spectrum of MnO2 NSs was due to the d-d leap of Mn (IV) ions in the MnO6 octahedron (Fig. 1 C) [43,44]. The Raman spectra of MnO2 NSs have two distinct peaks at 565.9 and 653.7 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of Mn-O, which was consistent with the literature (Fig. 1D) [45]. As shown in the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, 514 cm−1, 3427 cm−1, and 1627 cm−1 bands corresponded to the vibration of the octahedral [MnO6] frame, and the presence of H2O and H3O+ in MnO2 NSs, respectively (Fig. S1) [43]. The chemical composition and valence state of the materials before and after the reaction was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Figs. 1E and 1 F, the binding energy peaks of Mn 2p3/2 were affirmed to Mn (III) (640.5 eV) and Mn (IV) (641.4 eV and 642.7 eV) [46]. Meanwhile, the peak at 653.1 eV belongs to Mn 2p1/2. After the reaction, the XPS spectra of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 did not change significantly, indicating that MnO2 NSs was only reduced to Mn2+ (Fig. 1 G and 1 H). All the above characterization results indicate that the MnO2 NSs were prepared satisfactorily.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of dual-mode assay for BChE and parathion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical strategy for dual-mode detection of BChE is illustrated in Scheme 1. The strategy is divided into two main parts: (1) BChE can hydrolyze ATCh to produce the reductive TCh, which could induce decompose of the brown MnO2 NSs to colorless Mn2+. (2) After the selective separation of the reacted solution using a filter membrane, colorimetric detection was achieved with a smartphone by identifying the RGB value of the MnO2 NSs coated membrane, while the Mn2+ in the filtrate was detected by ICP-MS. The higher the activity of BChE, the more Mn2+ generated. Therefore, the concentration of BChE is proportional to the RGB signal and the ICP-MS signal. Since parathion is an inhibitor of BChE, the above strategy can also be used to detect parathion.

In order to verify the feasibility of the above strategy, the UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NSs in the presence of ATCh, BChE, and the mixture of ATCh and BChE were firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, only in the coexistence of ATCh, BChE, and MnO2 NSs, there was an obvious decline in absorption peak. Parathion can inhibit the activity of BChE, resulting in a rebound in the absorbance of MnO2 NSs. The RGB values of the filter membrane were analyzed using a smartphone after filtration. The ratios of R/(R + G + B), G/(R + G + B), and B/(R + G + B) of the filter membranes of MnO2 NSs were first investigated to gain the best sensitivity. Compared with R/(R + G + B) and B/(R + G + B), the G/(R + G + B) has relatively a high value (Fig. 2B). As shown in Fig. 2 C, it can be seen by the naked eye that ATCh or BChE does not cause color changing of MnO2 NSs from brown to light brown significantly. And RGB analysis also reveals there was no significant change in G/(R + G + B) values. However, the enzyme catalyzed reaction product TCh could cause the color of MnO2 NSs to become light and significantly increase the G/(R + G + B) values. On the contrary, the addition of parathion resulted in a relative darkening of the membrane with a decrease of G/(R + G + B) values. In addition, we also use SEM to investigate the microcosmic appearance of the filter membrane after filtration via adding different concentrations of BChE in the mixture. It can be seen from Fig. S2A-E that the BChE concentration is inversely proportional to the amount of MnO2 NSs on the filter membrane. The content of Mn2+ in the filtrate was detected by ICP-MS after filtration. The results show that the ICP-MS signal was basically unaffected by ATCh or BChE, and the signals were significantly enhanced by adding BChE and ATCh. Parathion, as an inhibitor to suppress the activity of BChE, could decrease the decomposition of MnO2 NSs, leading to the ICP-MS signal dropping back (Fig. 2D). Meanwhile, the MnO2 NSs were negatively charged (ζ = −22.07 mV), while Mn2+ were positively charged. In the presence of ATCh and BChE, the zeta potential showed a significant reduction from − 22.07 mV to − 14.50 mV (MnO2 NSs and Mn2+ mixture), indicating the decomposition of MnO2 NSs by TCh (Fig. S3). The above experimental results show that this dual-mode sensing strategy is feasible for detecting BChE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for BChE assay
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment conditions were optimized detailed including the effect of the concentration of ATCh, MnO2 NSs, the hydrolysis time of BChE, the reaction time with MnO2, and the inhibition time of BChE by parathion. Firstly, the concentration of ATCh was optimized, and the result shows that the ICP-MS signal value (Δ ICP-MS) and colorimetry signal G/(R + G + B) (Δ G/(R + G + B)) detected by the smartphone increased firstly and then decreased slowly, reaching a maximum at 5 mM (Fig. S4A-C). As shown in Fig. S4D-F, we could see the maximum Δ ICP-MS signal and Δ G/(R + G + B) signals at the MnO2 NSs concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The incubation time of BChE and ATCh had a significant effect on the analytical performance. As shown in Fig. S4G, different reaction times were optimized and the best time was 25 min. The reduction time between TCh and MnO2 NSs was optimized (Fig. S4H). The results show that 6 min can exhibit the best efficiency. In addition, parathion as an inhibitor of BChE, the effect of the reaction time of parathion and BChE has also been investigated in the range of 0–40 min, the maximum intensity was obtained at 30 min (Fig. S4I).

Under the optimal conditions, with the increase of BChE, the ICP-MS and G/(R + G + B) intensity gradually increased. Good linear relationship between absorbance and BChE concentration was gained in the range of 5–150 U/L (linear equation of Y = −0.0021 C + 0.38 (R 2 = 0.990)) with a LOD of 0.64 U/L (Figs. S5A and 5B). As shown in Fig. 3 A, moreover, there was also a color change of MnO2 NSs membrane from brown to colorless with the increase of BChE concentration in the range of 3–150 U/L at natural light, and the 3 U/L of BChE could be distinguished from the blank solution by the naked eye. Then RGB analysis was also performed for analysis of the filter membrane by smartphone. A good linear was observed between G/(R + G + B) and BChE activity in the range of 3–150 U/L (Fig. 3B). The linear equation was Y = 0.00034 C + 0.31 (R 2 = 0.996) with a LOD and RSD (n = 7) of 1.3 U/L and 1.6%, respectively. Finally, the content of Mn2+ in the filtrate was detected by ICP-MS (Fig. 3 C). The linear equation was Y = 340.61 C + 180.12 (linear range: 0.005–0.2 U/L). The LOD and RSD (n = 7) were calculated as 0.001 U/L and 1.1%, respectively. After comparing the analytical performance with those of existing techniques for BChE assay (Table S2), a lower LOD was obtained of this developed sensing platform.

To evaluate the selectivity of the method, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin, cellulase, amylase, pepsin, pectinase, glutathione (GSH), L -cysteine ( L -Cys), and ascorbic acid (AA) were selected as potential interference at 10 times the concentration of BChE (60 U/L). As shown in Fig. 3D, there were unapparent interferences after adding these substances, except for GSH, L -Cys, and AA. However, the interference of GSH or L-Cys could be effectively eliminated by a special thiols scavenger N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) [47]. As displayed in Fig. 3D, after incubation of GSH or Cys with NEM (0.3 M), the R/(R+G+B) ratio exhibited insignificant changes in contrast to the control. The normal level of AA in the serum sample was 22.71–85.17 μM. In this work, the sample solution was diluted 100 times before detection due to the high concentration of BChE in the serum. Therefore, we study the influence by adding 1 μM AA on MnO2 sensing platform (Fig. 3D), and the signal change of AA (μM) was similar to that of the blank sample. The results indicated that the method has a strong anti-interference ability for the BChE assay, which may by reason of the specific hydrolysis of ATCh by BChE.

To assess the usefulness and accuracy of the proposed sensing platform, BChE was quantified in clinical serum samples from patients with liver disease. As shown in Table 1, 40 samples were tested using this method, and the results were in accordance with the clinical results where 4900 U/L is the clinical cutoff value ( Fig. 4 A). The spiking recoveries of the proposed method were in the range of 91.7–104.1% with RSD (n = 3) values of < 6.4% (Table S3). The color of the filter membrane can also be seen to be darker in patients with positive liver disease (Fig. 4B). For example, NO.4 and 5 samples with BChE activity below 4900 U/L were clinically diagnosed as positive for liver disease and consistent results were obtained using this method for the assay. Therefore, the above results indicate that the analytical method is suitable for the BChE activity assay in clinical serum samples.

Parathion, as an inhibitor of BChE, can also be quantitatively analyzed via this dual-mode strategy. After the inhibition of BChE by parathion, a gradual increase of UV–vis absorbance can be observed in the range of parathion concentration of 3–70 μg/mL with a good linear equation Y = 0.0025 C + 0.20 (R 2 = 0.996) and a LOD of 1.1 μg/mL (Fig. S6A and 6B). A gradual darkening of the membrane in the range of 3–70 μg/mL can be seen after filter-assisted separation, and the naked eye can distinguish the 3 μg/mL parathion from the blank solution ( Fig. 5 A). RGB analysis showed that the G/(R + G + B) signal has a good linear relationship (Y = −0.00071 C + 0.36 (R 2 = 0.996)) with parathion, and the LOD and RSD (n = 7) were 1.6 μg/mL and 1.2%, respectively (Fig. 5B). As the parathion increased, ICP-MS (Fig. 5 C) signals of Mn2+ decreased gradually, showing good linear range (0.05–10 ng/mL), and the LOD was 0.028 ng/mL, which are better or comparable to those of the reported methods (Table S4).

The selectivity was also tested by adding 6 enzymes and common electrolytes (Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+), respectively. A significant signal was gained when low content of parathion (30 μg/ mL) was measured, whereas the RGB responses were unapparent in the presence of high content of interferences (300 μg/ mL; Fig. 5D).

In conclusion, we presented a convenient, rapid, and sensitive dual-mode method for BChE assay in clinical samples. The LODs were as low as 1.3 U/L and 0.001 U/L for smartphone-based RGB and ICP-MS analysis. The monitor results of 40 clinical samples of liver disease were highly compliance with clinical diagnosis. As the technique is simple, sensitive, and specific uses without expensive reagents by colorimetry detection, which may have a broad application prospect in the diagnosis of clinical liver diseases.

Qiuyue Yu: performed the experiments and wrote the manuscript, Piaopiao Chen and Ke Huang: designed and guided the experiments, and revised the manuscript, Xin Yuan: performed the synthesis and characterization of MnO2 and data processing, Qi Guo: performed clinical experiments, Jinrong Zhou: revised the original draft of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21605108), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2021YFS0232). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132472.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure and therapeutic uses of butyrylcholinesterase: application in detoxification, Alzheimer's disease, and fat metabolism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xing, Q. Li, B. Xiong, Y. Chen, F. Feng, W. Liu, H. Sun, Structure and therapeutic uses of butyrylcholinesterase: Application in detoxification, Alzheimer's disease, and fat metabolism, Med. Res. Rev. 41 (2021) 858–901.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ generation of Prussian Blue by MIL-53 (Fe) for point-of-care testing of butyrylcholinesterase activity using a portable high-throughput photothermal device

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Guo, Y.J. Zhang, Y.L. Yu, J.H. Wang, In situ generation of Prussian Blue by MIL-53 (Fe) for point-of-care testing of butyrylcholinesterase activity using a portable high-throughput photothermal device, Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 14806–14813.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Significance of preoperative butyrylcholinesterase as an independent predictor of survival in patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer treated with radical cystectomy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Koie, C. Ohyama, S. Hatakeyama, A. Imai, T. Yoneyama, Y. Hashimoto, Significance of preoperative butyrylcholinesterase as an independent predictor of survival in patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer treated with radical cystectomy, J. Clin. Oncol. 34 (2016) 373–373.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for discriminative and highly sensitive detection of acetylcholinesterase and butyrylcholinesterase in human whole blood

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, Y. Cen, G. Xu, F. Wei, M. Shi, Q. Hu, A ratiometric fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for discriminative and highly sensitive detection of acetylcholinesterase and butyrylcholinesterase in human whole blood, Biosens. Bioelectron. 131 (2019) 232–236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Butyrylcholinesterase as a prognostic marker: a review of the literature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Santarpia, I. Grandone, F. Contaldo, F. Pasanisi, Butyrylcholinesterase as a prognostic marker: A review of the literature, J. Cachexia Sarcopeni. 4 (2013) 31–39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preety, Bio-sensing of organophosphorus pesticides: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Pundir, A. Malik, Preety, Bio-sensing of organophosphorus pesticides: A review, Biosens. Bioelectron. 140 (2019) 111348.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new and rapid colorimetric determination of acetylcholinesterase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.L. Ellman, K.D. Courtney, V. Andres, Jr., R.M. Feather-Stone, A new and rapid colorimetric determination of acetylcholinesterase activity, Biochem. Pharmacol. 7 (1961) 88–95.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric methods based on 2,6-dichloroindophenol acetate and indoxylacetate for butyrylcholinesterase activity assay in plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pohanka, L. Drtinova, Spectrophotometric methods based on 2,6-dichloroindophenol acetate and indoxylacetate for butyrylcholinesterase activity assay in plasma, Talanta 106 (2013) 281–285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of dual biomarkers from humans exposed to organophosphorus pesticides by combination of immunochromatographic test strip and ellman assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yang, Y. Zhao, L. Wang, M. Paulsen, C.D. Simpson, F. Liu, D. Du, Y. Lin, Simultaneous detection of dual biomarkers from humans exposed to organophosphorus pesticides by combination of immunochromatographic test strip and ellman assay, Biosens. Bioelectron. 104 (2018) 39–44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-readout immunochromatographic assay by utilizing MnO2 nanoflowers as the unique colorimetric/chemiluminescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ouyang, Q. Lu, W. Wang, Y. Song, X. Tu, C. Zhu, J.N. Smith, D. Du, Z. Fu, Y. Lin, Dual-readout immunochromatographic assay by utilizing MnO2 nanoflowers as the unique colorimetric/chemiluminescent probe, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 5147–5152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative butyrylcholinesterase activity detection by surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nechaeva, T. Prokopkina, G. Makhaeva, E. Rudakova, N. Boltneva, C. Dishovsky, A. Eremenko, I. Kurochkin, Quantitative butyrylcholinesterase activity detection by surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 259 (2018) 75–82.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic electrochemical sensing platform for biomonitoring of exposure to organophosphorus pesticides and nerve agents based on simultaneous measurement of total enzyme amount and enzyme activity

Su, Single-atom iron containing nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity and copper nanoclusters based ratio fluorescent strategy for acetylcholinesterase activity sensing, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 313 (2020) 128023.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qiuyue Yu is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward biosensors.
Qi Guo is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward element speciation.
Jinrong Zhou is a master’s student in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. She is currently working toward colorimetric sensing.

Xin Yuan received her BSc degree in 2010 from the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Ph.D. degree in 2015 in analytical chemistry from Sichuan University. She began her own independent career in the College of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Her current research interests are analytical instruments and their application.

Ke Huang received his BSc degree in 2010 from the College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Ph.D. degree in 2015 in analytical chemistry from the College of Chemistry, Sichuan University. He began his own independent career in the College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University. His current research interest is analytical spectroscopy.
Piaopiao Chen is an assistant professor in West China Hospital, Sichuan University. His current research interest is analytical spectroscopy and new techniques for clinical diagnosis.
